speaker: interviewer
text: --Goldman. We're in Sutton, Surrey, England. This is tape five. You were saying about the place at the Polish university--

speaker: interviewee
text: University course. This was created by the Polish government, the ex-government, who had some funds. And they wanted to make use of it, something, so they opened a Polish university for ex-servicemen to bridge them, to get them through to-- to English university. Because most of-- you had to have matriculation or some documents that you were studying before. Or even if you finished studying, and you want-- you had to redo it here, this was a sort of introduction. So the one year was in Polish and in English. And to-- to prepare you for entrance examination to London University. At that time, you could get a simplified entrance only for a subject. And [INAUDIBLE]-- I didn't know about it, but she explained to me. When I went to visit her, she explained to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the few apples that I [? owe-- ?] the two, three apples which I did in a gesture because I saw-- came back in a very, very constructive way. Because for me, this was another way to go back to education, which was very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think I have a chance, because most of the people who were going there were university element. Either university or at least-- minimal-- which I had neither. I had a very scattered sort of little few months there and back in Poland, and a few-- a little short period in-- in Russian school, which level was mostly political. Their educational system was completely different. But a little bit of smattering, so I had very little, really. I didn't have. But this was-- so I didn't think I have a chance. But he encouraged me and said, well-- he gave me some papers from previous exam-- try it. And they were then experimenting with intelligence tests. And I think they knew very little about them, and I managed to get a place. And they were mostly men-- ex-servicemen. This is where, in a way, I met my husband, who was legitimate there, because he had matriculated in Warsaw. And he was doing law, but he never liked law. He wanted to do science, physics. And we met there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this university?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- near Kensington. Gosh, the station before Kensington, with the theatre? Go on, help me. Next to Kensington. It will come back. Royal Court.

speaker: interviewer
text: Royal Court. Sloane Square.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sloane Square. I remember the theatre. Sloane Square. They had a building, and it was there. And we lived, at that time, in a room in Earls Court. And we were both students. My husband-- I chose-- he chose physical, because he wanted to. I chose science for matriculation. I took physics, mathematics, applied mathematics, because you needed very little English for that, and my English was almost nonexistent. But I had to pass also in English. And I managed to do that. And then, because London University was very overcrowded-- but we managed to get to Woolwich, which was an internal college. And because my husband did physics-- I did chemistry. I didn't know really what I wanted to do. And I studied. But studying was good for me at that period, because it occupied me mentally. You know, it gave me sort of a buffer. And it was a challenge. And I never liked chemistry, and I was hopeless in chemistry. It was the last subject you could choose, because it's really memory. You have to remember. I was-- mathematics, I was [INAUDIBLE] very good at it, because it's a logical subject. Once you understand it, you can progress. But with chemistry, if I didn't have the foundation and I was doing a university course, you can imagine the disaster. Meanwhile, they also found that I had TB glands. I also-- my husband was a few years older, and I wanted a family. I was hoping to-- that giving birth will be such a tremendous experience it just will wipe up everything which happened behind. It doesn't. It produces more problems. I mean, it is, in a way, a life-giving experience. But-- so I interrupted my study. I did my intermediate. I never finished, and I don't regret it. Chemistry was never my subject. And I had my first son in 1949, Martin. And then, very soon, '51, I had my other son, Stephen. We lived-- we had a terrible time in poverty, because while we were students, we had a grant. And that was bearable. We could maintain ourselves. Later on, my husband couldn't find a job. It was a difficult labor market at that time before Korea. And he went to be a porter at Euston Station. And he was a very bad porter. But eventually he got a job with the British Oxygen Company, and this is why we came to south of London, because their research establishment was in Morden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my black period really started after giving birth to the children. You know, now I read there is a sort of aft-- post-- postal depression? Something-- Postnatal depression.

speaker: interviewee
text: Postnatal. Whether this was part of that, or whether this was also a time that I really had time to think what happened to me. I was-- you know, I didn't have the challenge of-- of-- of intellectual university studying. I didn't have the survival instinct. So this was a time where I was thawing out, and it was a very black period. You would think that having two small babies, that this will give me a pivot and strength and it will occupy me. It did in one way, but another way, it also realized the gap and the emptiness and missing the family. And, you know, bringing up children without a grandmother, without aunt, without cousin, it created some problems, too. And also, we were very poor. We struggled this all together. And I had a sort of emotional vomiting, at that time. Obviously it had to come out one way or another. I had a very nice psychiatrist who-- an outpatient-- and he recommended psychoanalysis. But psychoanalysis depend-- you have to pay for it. This is the relationship. And I couldn't afford it, so I dropped it. But he was very supportive. But there was a history of that going on in the background, all the time. And I really started recovering more when I started working, because there, again, I had a discipline. I had an order from-- I had to do certain things. I had to be time. And I didn't-- I didn't qualify in anything, so I didn't have an entrance anywhere. But I managed to get a local job with a local store. And very soon, within a very short time, I got a buyership, which usually takes years. But I managed to do it within the year. And I had an interesting career, in a way, because it was local. I lived locally. I worked local-- I mean, I lived in Sutton, by that time-- from Morden to Sutton. The job in Sutton.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I could combine the job with being a mother, a wife, and a career. I also joined the community, because then I could afford it. Before, I couldn't afford joining.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Wimbledon-- you know, this is one of the things they told me-- you can send your children even if you're not a member. And this is where we sent Martin and Stephen, to Wimbledon, to Sunday school. But when we moved to Sutton, it was logical to have local, because I thought they should have local friends. It never gelled, somehow, but this was the reason why I joined locally. And my husband was working at British Oxygen Company. He finished his BSE in physics. And he did his PhD in his-- when he was already working and he had the job in British Oxygen Company. So life was normalizing. I started working. But there was this black period when I had the small children cooped up, and we lived in the council estate. And struggling with establishing yourself and-- which, in poverty, in a way. And it was tough. I had all the sort of emotional upheaval. And-- but it's with me all the time, in a way. And then-- I don't know whether this is of any interest-- my husband died in 1972. Heart-- he was slim. He didn't smoke. He didn't have any of the things which one usually connects with it, but it happened. He had his first attack about eight years before, and he died in December, 1972.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I had to reorganize my life again. I left my job. Of course, I could have stayed to work. I needed a new challenge. And I got another job as a buyer-designer in fashion jewelry. It meant traveling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was, again, the curiosity came with that. Traveling abroad and Far East offered some kind of excitement. And the job was very tough. Anything connected with fashion. I mean, I really didn't qualify for any of it, because in the store, I used to do interior designing, lighting, and china and glass. I didn't have any qualifications. But instinct, common sense, and applying yourself-- at that time it was easy. Now, you need qualification. But that time, you could-- you could do it. And so the job, in the last 10 years of my career, was traveling to the Far East. And, again, my curiosity came to the front, because most businessmen go from airport to the office and back, but I used to combine it with 10 days' holiday-- a week and weekend. And I went-- I had the opportunity of traveling, maybe in a very superficial way, but nonetheless coming in contact with different cultures, different religion. I went to Japan and China and India and Malaysia and Australia in a sort of very superficial way, but it does give you a glimpse of a different-- you know, we sort of think Europe is the center of the world. There is a Far East, which is developing very quickly and very well and very-- the Chinese are very intelligent people. And the Japanese.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a little bit of that. And then when I reached 60, I decided that I have time. I did my calculation, and I said, I can live on my-- what I have. And I wanted to have a little bit of leisure. It's the first time in my life that I wanted to have some leisure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that, in my period from '72 to '86, I was very involved in the community. I was president of B'nai B'rith, involved in WIZO and Ladies' Guild, all these usual commitments. I wanted to prove that you can do all that, be a career woman, be a wife, be a-- already a-- not widow-- and a mother. When I retired, I decided that I want to relinquish all these activities. I want to be a private person and to recoup, you know, the lost years in my education. And the way I decided to do this, through art. I mean, I didn't have any contact with art, because I had more scientific education. But the facilities in-- here in London, we are so lucky. We have so many. Traveling to town, for my age group, is free. National Gallery is free, Tate, and British Museum. And it's a source of education, you really-- and through-- through art, you can fill in with mythology, history, all kinds of things. You can go through art. You'll find your way. History. And it's a fantastic way. And I-- I-- I have the best days of my life, in a way. Of course, I didn't mention the tragedy which happened to me in 1984, where I lost my son. I thought my cup was full when I came from the survival. But you can never say that. Then I lost my husband in '72, and I lost my son in '84.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was Martin. How was he killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: You never-- you never get over things like that. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Martin die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mart-- in a stupid train accident. By that time, he was a producer of science programs with the Scottish BBC, and he lived in Scotland. And his wife, Frances, was a lecturer, and is still a lecturer in Glasgow University. And they lived in Glasgow. And he was commuting between Glasgow and Edinburgh, and there was a railway derailment. And I was, at that time, in Hong Kong when I heard the news. And I came back the 16 hours or 14 hours on the, oh, on the plane. And, well, I honestly think I must be very strong and very tough. I remember my mother used to say, in the 1920s when she met some refugees from Russia, and there was a young woman, she said she told these stories, terrible stories, what happened. How they were shooting. How her parents were shot in front of her. How she was raped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was she with us dancing and laughing. And I said, she must be an animal. How can she do it? I look at myself, and I think, how can I do it? You know? It's-- it's-- it's with me all the time, but I try very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Either you go under-- and I've seen people who have done that-- or you-- you-- your aim is not to become a problem for the people around you. You know, for Stephen and his family, for my grandchildren. So you try very hard to-- to continue the best you can. Stephen and Nicole were a great support, because one of the main reason of my purpose of my survival, and what I remember, were the last words of my mother-- 'Remember who you are.' And I try to remember. I try to remember the traditions of my family and the-- the-- the behavior and the ethics of my family background. And it was very difficult to do it, because by losing your god in the religion, to become-- to remain Jewish without a family support-- you know, Judaism is very much a family support. You have the Shabbat, and you have the Pesach with the family together. We didn't have that. We didn't have the family-- I didn't have the family support.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I-- my husband was agnostic, in a way. So he used to, sort of, in a way, jokes, what's the point of you lighting the candle if you go to work on Saturday? Because I work-- there was no logic in it. It was-- I'd-- I'm-- I'm not religious, but culturally I feel very Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And because I paid such a high price for my Jewish survival. The Jewish continuity was fundamental to me. It was-- it was one of the things that, without it, I don't think I could cope. And this is why I'm-- I appreciate very much what Nicole has done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say what that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she-- she was-- she's French. And she converted. She had the wisdom of understanding what it means for the family, and-- and the children are now part of the Jewish community. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I feel very, very relaxed about it. And why am I crying? [LAUGHS] [CRYING] Oh, hopeless, hopeless.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I feel very grateful, because without it, it would have been much harder. My grandchildren are very proud of being Jewish. They go to King's, and my grandson looks at the menu, and if there are sausages [LAUGHTER] which are not kosher, ham, he doesn't have it. And Nicole decided that-- in her conversion, the rabbi told her, if she does one thing well, it's more important than to do a lot of things and not doing well. So she excluded pork and ham as not part of the household. Of the kashrut-- this is her contribution to the kashrut. And the children take it. You know, and I feel very proud, because they are proud Jewish. They enjoy being part of the community. And to me, this is fundamental. I'm very grateful for that, because without it, I wouldn't find life-- very unbearable. Because it was the last words of my mother's. And it was very difficult to impose it. I mean, my difficulty with Frances was that-- that I was hoping, again, that she will understand and she will respond to it. But I didn't find this response in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is why I find it-- I'm very appreciative that I have-- that we have Shabbat. We meet every Friday, once in my place, once at their place. We have Pesach. We have a tradition, our own.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe my grandfather and my father wouldn't approve of that, because they were very orthodox. Nonetheless, I think I would explain to them, and I think they would appreciate that. And I-- I'm very pleased that I have that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I really mean it that it's very fundamental to my well-being. To-- to-- I'm more relaxed. I hope that my other grandchildren-- Rebecca and Jonathan, who don't have the link with the Jewish community, who don't have the education, don't know very much about it-- that perhaps, in time, they will find-- out of curiosity-- they will learn something about it. I always feel, why should they know all about Jesus Christ and not to know about Moses? I hope that they will.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are beautiful children. Very bright, very intelligent. And if Martin was alive, I know that he would want them to continue. I know that every Friday he would phone me up, and Rebecca, the three-year-old, would phone me also to wish me Shabbat shalom. So I know that there would have been a link. But I accept the situation, because Frances has their-- has her own attitude and her own way of bringing up the children. The children are hers, and I have to accept it. We-- being in Glasgow, it's also far. I don't have the close link with them. But they are beautiful children. Very bright, very intelligent. And I hope that they will be part of our family as long as I am alive, and perhaps after that, too. I hope that they will learn, as they go along.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wouldn't-- I never wanted to impose, you know? One of the things which I hope is to-- especially as now I am learning about different religion, accepting different points of view, different outlooks. But in my case, it was a special case, because, as the only survivor of the family, I felt the responsibility, the almost-- the demand, of continuing. Otherwise, my survival would be meaningless. It is only meaningful if there is a continuation, and I'm very glad that I've got it and I appreciate it. Tell me the names of Nicole and Stephen's children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's Daniel. He's 15. He had a bar mitzvah already. Then there's David, who had, this year, a bar mitzvah. And there's Joseph. [LAUGHS] A very nice biblical name. Joseph is nine. And they are beautiful three children. And they give me lots of pleasure. I have a lot to be grateful for. I'm looking for some water. It's all right. Fine. Thank you. I've got it. [DRINKS] They are lovely. They're-- they're-- they are normal. All the time, I'm looking out, whether it was my children or my grandchildren, that they are normal, that they don't carry the scars. Because you can so easily overdo it. And I see it in other families. And I see it especially when I go to Israel. I see it. You know, sometimes, from best intention, people can overdo it. And it's-- it's-- it's important, you know, that they-- they grow up normal. They learn in it in an educational way without scarring, without damaging their thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very difficult. It was very difficult to-- to-- to lead a normal-- I never felt normal, you know? I always felt-- and even now, I still have nightmares. And even now, when I come in a room. You know, I avoided all the sort of big bar mitzvahs and big weddings, because whenever I come in a big room, I've got the image of the synagogue and the crowd there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's with me all the time. And it's-- it's-- it's difficult to fit in into a normal life to-- with my background. And I found it difficult all along to pretend. There is always this duality. But I think-- until today, I thought I managed it much better. But because maybe you are such a sympathetic listener, it's just one of those things. Because we come, and it touches this sore point which comes out. But normally, I couldn't have done it-- I haven't done it well today-- but I couldn't have done it years before. But now I thought that I'm-- I'm-- I might-- I've learned to live with it, that I can tell the story. But yet, it's painful. It's-- it's-- it's-- it's full of sadness and pain. But at the same time, there is a lot of goodness, too. I mean, all the people in my life who helped me at one stage were good people. And I don't want to believe-- to lose the belief in the goodness of man, because otherwise, again, life would be unbearable. There is this power for good and bad in each of us. And it depends which one wins, which one is projected or encouraged or-- or-- and I'm a firm believer that only through education can you do it. I'm now very much converted to art. I think that art has got a great inference to play in-- in-- in bringing up a better world. And I know science-- we have so many fallen idols on the way. Also, I had a very scientific background, and I was educated. You know, if you saw the consequences of scientific effort in the camps, you just realize how dangerous science can be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message that you'd like your grandchildren to have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just that I love them very much. [LAUGHS] That I hope that they'll remember-- that they will continue the way they are. And that they will-- I see in Daniel a great interest, really, and David, as they're growing up. They want to know, and so I'm encouraged by that. And that they will have a fulfilled life, that they will grow up as nice as they are doing now, that they'll be nice human beings. That's very important for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there anything else that-- that you feel that we may not have covered? Anything else you feel is important to say before we finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, more or less I think I mentioned it that-- that very often in these memories, I hope that I don't-- that I represent a generation, that I don't talk only about myself. Because whether I was lucky or unlucky, survived, that-- you know, if somebody said, oh, aren't you lucky you survived? You know, sometimes it's like a knife into-- what is the luck to survive if you just survived on your own? There is not great luck in it. I think the-- the-- the consequences of the survival afterwards are just as grim, as terrible, as this process of surviving sometimes. Because while you were surviving, you were like a hunted animal. You didn't stop and think. You didn't have time to analyze. You didn't think what was happening. You were just like a hunted animal, and every corner was dangerous. And you didn't know what was happening a minute after. I never believed I'll survive. But the living afterwards, living with the memories of the past, living with-- with everything that happened before to you and trying to fit in into a normal life is very difficult. I found it very difficult. Because these experiences either brutalize you, and then you become very tough, and I've seen it happening. But if you remain sensitive, then it becomes very difficult. I'm sometimes glad that I'm capable of crying, that I'm capable of lamenting. There is nothing wrong with it. Maybe it's uncomfortable for people to look, but-- but it proves to me that I remain human after all that. Because it's so easily-- it can so easily sway in both direction.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's-- it's-- it's important, from my point of view, not only as a historical document, what you're doing, but also, from my own point of view, to-- to-- it gives a certain relief of having it. And also as a document for my children, so I appreciate very much what is being done. And I think it's done very well, very tactfully. And thank you very much. I just want to ask you one more thing, which was to do with your-- your Jewish faith and having started it. You said you lost your faith along the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a specific time or place where you lost your faith, or if it was gradual?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think on the train when I was sitting. These good people. You know, we were really good people. Maybe too submissive, but this was the conditioning of a generation and generation. When I saw my grandmother in that situation, these innocent people being subjected to this kind-- I didn't know what was going to happen, but just this stage, you know, with the three days without food and this kind of situation, without any sanitation. And also, I just couldn't believe that there could be a god who could see all that and-- and allow it to happen. I just-- I just-- it never occurred to me before. I never questioned it, because, I say, I was brought up in a very-- but gradually, gradually, I just find-- I mean, religion should have a beneficial-- should-- should do-- give the people a moral code, an ethical. And it can do that. But at the same time, if you see what happened in the name of religion, you begin to doubt it. I mean, all the anti-Semitism which-- Hitler succeeded because he was on fertile ground. He had this element there. And the origin of this anti-Semitic feeling comes from Catholic preaching, from Catholicism. I was made responsible for crucifixion of Christ, even as a child in school. At Easter time, I was afraid to go out in the street, because I was made responsible for the crucifixion. They'd forgotten that Jesus was a Jew. So-- so-- so you just realize, you know, that religion can have a very beneficial. It needed it, you know, to-- it can have a very good effect. But also, what has been done in the name of religion is-- is very-- very catastrophic, too. [INAUDIBLE].
